items.prompt_md: Why must the loop condition check **both** `fast` and `fast.next` (not just `fast`)?
options: Because `fast.next.next` would fail on a `None` node when `fast.next` is already `None`
To make the slow pointer move faster
It's only needed when the list is empty
Because Python requires two conditions in every `while` loop
items.answer: Because `fast.next.next` would fail on a `None` node when `fast.next` is already `None`
items.explanation_md: `fast` advances two nodes per iteration, so before computing `fast.next.next` both `fast` and `fast.next` must exist — skipping either check risks an attribute access on `None`.

items.prompt_md: Why does the fast/slow pointer approach need only a single pass, unlike "count the length, then walk to n // 2"?
options: Because `fast` covers twice the distance `slow` does, so the moment `fast` exhausts the list, `slow` has necessarily covered exactly half — no separate counting pass needed
Because it skips visiting half of the nodes entirely
Because the list is assumed to already be sorted
Because `slow` and `fast` start at different nodes
items.answer: Because `fast` covers twice the distance `slow` does, so the moment `fast` exhausts the list, `slow` has necessarily covered exactly half — no separate counting pass needed
items.explanation_md: The 2:1 speed ratio is what ties `fast` finishing to `slow` being exactly at the midpoint, collapsing what would otherwise be two passes into one.

items.prompt_md: Why does copying every node's value into an array just to index the middle waste something the fast/slow sweep doesn't?
options: It wastes O(n) extra space holding every value, when the fast/slow sweep only ever needs two pointer variables
It's actually faster than fast/slow pointers
Arrays can't store values from a linked list
It requires the list to be reversed first
items.answer: It wastes O(n) extra space holding every value, when the fast/slow sweep only ever needs two pointer variables
items.explanation_md: The array approach still finds the middle correctly, but it pays O(n) space for something the two-speed pointer trick achieves in O(1) extra space.

items.prompt_md: How can the same two-speed pointer idea used to find a midpoint also detect a cycle in a linked list?
options: If there's a cycle, `fast` and `slow` will eventually land on the exact same node inside the loop, since `fast` gains one node of relative distance on `slow` every step
A cycle makes `fast` run off the end of the list immediately
Cycle detection requires a completely different data structure, like a hash set, and fast/slow cannot do it
Fast/slow pointers only work on lists with no cycles at all
items.answer: If there's a cycle, `fast` and `slow` will eventually land on the exact same node inside the loop, since `fast` gains one node of relative distance on `slow` every step
items.explanation_md: Inside a cycle, `fast` closes the gap on `slow` by one node per iteration, so they're guaranteed to meet — the loop condition changes from "until fast runs out" to "until fast equals slow."

items.prompt_md: To find the node that is `k` positions from the end of a singly linked list in one pass, how is the fast/slow idea adapted?
options: Advance `fast` alone by `k` nodes first, then move `slow` and `fast` together one step at a time until `fast` reaches the end — `slow` is then k nodes from the end
Advance both pointers together from the very first step, at the same speed
Reverse the list, walk k nodes from the new head, then reverse it back
Count the total length twice, once for each pointer
items.answer: Advance `fast` alone by `k` nodes first, then move `slow` and `fast` together one step at a time until `fast` reaches the end — `slow` is then k nodes from the end
items.explanation_md: Giving `fast` a k-node head start turns the fixed gap between the pointers into the distance from `slow` to the end, so when `fast` finishes, `slow` sits exactly k nodes back — all in one pass, no length counted.

items.prompt_md: In an even-length list, which node does the standard `while fast and fast.next` loop land `slow` on: the first middle or the second middle?
options: The second middle, because `fast` exhausts the list one step after passing the true center
The first middle, always
It alternates unpredictably based on the values stored
Neither — it lands on the last node
items.answer: The second middle, because `fast` exhausts the list one step after passing the true center
items.explanation_md: For a 4-node list, `fast` reaches the end exactly when `slow` has taken 2 steps, landing on the second of the two middle nodes; starting `slow` one step ahead of `fast` instead would land on the first middle.

items.prompt_md: Why is fast/slow pointers a better fit than counting the length up front when the list is effectively a stream (e.g., nodes generated lazily and not fully known ahead of time)?
options: Fast/slow only needs to observe the list once, discovering the midpoint as a byproduct of a single walk, with no need to know the total length beforehand
Streams cannot be walked more than once under any approach
Counting the length is always faster for streams
Fast/slow requires the length to be known in advance too
items.answer: Fast/slow only needs to observe the list once, discovering the midpoint as a byproduct of a single walk, with no need to know the total length beforehand
items.explanation_md: The two-pass "count then walk to n // 2" approach fundamentally needs the length before it can compute where to stop; fast/slow sidesteps that requirement entirely.

items.prompt_md: What edge case does `while fast and fast.next` correctly handle for a single-node list (`head.next is None`)?
options: The loop body never executes because `fast.next` is `None`, so `slow` stays at `head` — correctly, since a one-node list's middle is itself
It raises an exception on a one-node list
It infinite-loops on a one-node list
It requires a special `if len(list) == 1` branch before the loop
items.answer: The loop body never executes because `fast.next` is `None`, so `slow` stays at `head` — correctly, since a one-node list's middle is itself
items.explanation_md: With only one node, `fast.next` is `None` immediately, so the guard is false from the start and `slow` never moves — it's already sitting on the only node, which is exactly right.

items.prompt_md: Why can't `slow`'s distance traveled ever exceed half of `fast`'s when the loop terminates, regardless of odd or even list length?
options: Because each loop iteration advances `slow` by exactly one node and `fast` by exactly two, so `slow`'s total steps are always half of `fast`'s total steps, however many iterations run
Because `slow` sometimes skips iterations
Because the list length is always assumed even
Because `fast` moves one node and `slow` moves two
items.answer: Because each loop iteration advances `slow` by exactly one node and `fast` by exactly two, so `slow`'s total steps are always half of `fast`'s total steps, however many iterations run
items.explanation_md: The 1:2 step ratio holds every single iteration, so it holds in total no matter when the loop happens to stop — that invariant is the entire reason this pattern finds the midpoint at all.

items.prompt_md: Why would using two independent counters and walking the list twice (once to measure, once to seek) be considered strictly worse than fast/slow pointers here?
options: It touches the list twice as many times (2n node visits instead of about n), for no benefit when a single well-coordinated pass achieves the same result
It's asymptotically slower — O(n^2) instead of O(n)
It uses less memory than fast/slow pointers
It doesn't actually find the correct middle node
items.answer: It touches the list twice as many times (2n node visits instead of about n), for no benefit when a single well-coordinated pass achieves the same result
items.explanation_md: Both are O(n) asymptotically, but the two-pass version does strictly more traversal work and requires the list to be re-walkable — a real cost when a single fast/slow sweep gets the same answer in one visit per node.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the fast/slow pointer sweep to find the middle node of an n-node list?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: `fast` covers the whole list in roughly n/2 iterations, each doing constant work — a single linear pass, so O(n) overall.

items.prompt_md: What is the extra space complexity of the fast/slow pointer sweep, not counting the input list?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: Only two pointer variables (`slow`, `fast`) are used no matter how long the list is — constant extra space.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the "count the length first, then walk to node n // 2" two-pass approach to finding the middle?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Both passes are linear (one to count, one to seek), and two linear passes summed is still O(n) — asymptotically the same as fast/slow, though with more total traversal work.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of copying every node's value into an array before indexing the middle, for an n-node list?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: The array holds all n values simultaneously — this is exactly the extra space the fast/slow two-pointer sweep avoids.

items.prompt_md: Using fast/slow pointers to detect a cycle in an n-node list (Floyd's algorithm) costs what time complexity in the worst case?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Whether or not a cycle exists, the pointers meet or `fast` exhausts the list within a number of steps proportional to n — still linear time, with O(1) extra space.

items.prompt_md: You need the middle node of a singly linked list in a single traversal, without knowing its length beforehand. Which approach fits?
options: Fast & slow pointers
Reverse pointers in place
Dummy head + merge
Copy nodes to an array first
items.answer: Fast & slow pointers
items.explanation_md: "Find the midpoint, one pass, length unknown up front" is the direct trigger for the two-speed pointer sweep.

items.prompt_md: You must determine whether a singly linked list contains a cycle, using O(1) extra space. Which approach fits?
options: Fast & slow pointers, checking whether they ever land on the same node
Hash set of visited nodes
Dummy head + merge
Reverse pointers in place
items.answer: Fast & slow pointers, checking whether they ever land on the same node
items.explanation_md: O(1) space rules out the hash-set-of-visited-nodes approach; the two-speed pointer sweep detects a cycle by having `fast` inevitably catch up to `slow` inside the loop.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the node that is exactly `k` positions from the end of a singly linked list, in one pass, without knowing the list's total length ahead of time. Which approach fits?
options: Give one pointer a k-node head start, then advance both pointers together until the lead pointer reaches the end
Reverse pointers in place
Dummy head + merge
Copy nodes to an array first
items.answer: Give one pointer a k-node head start, then advance both pointers together until the lead pointer reaches the end
items.explanation_md: This is a variant of the two-pointer gap trick: a fixed head-start pointer preserves a constant distance, so when the lead pointer runs out, the trailing pointer is exactly k nodes from the end.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted ARRAY (not a linked list) and need to check whether any two elements sum to a target value, in O(n) time and O(1) extra space. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers starting at each end of the sorted array, moving inward based on the sum comparison
Fast & slow pointers
Dummy head + merge
Hash set of visited nodes
items.answer: Two pointers starting at each end of the sorted array, moving inward based on the sum comparison
items.explanation_md: Trap: this has a "two pointers moving" flavor like fast/slow, but a sorted array with random access calls for opposite-end pointers converging based on comparisons, not a same-direction speed differential meant for linked structures.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted array of integers and asked whether any value repeats, and you want a single pass with no sorting. Which approach fits?
options: Keep a hash set of values seen so far, checking membership before each insert
Fast & slow pointers
Dummy head + merge
Reverse pointers in place
items.answer: Keep a hash set of values seen so far, checking membership before each insert
items.explanation_md: Trap: "single pass" might suggest a pointer-speed trick, but detecting a duplicate value (not a structural cycle) is a membership question — a hash set is the correct tool, not fast/slow pointers.

items.prompt_md: You have a sorted array and need to find the middle index to use as a pivot for binary search. Which approach fits for finding that middle position?
options: Compute the index directly as `(left + right) // 2` using random access
Fast & slow pointers
Dummy head + merge
Reverse pointers in place
items.answer: Compute the index directly as `(left + right) // 2` using random access
items.explanation_md: Trap: "find the middle" cues the fast/slow midpoint pattern, but an array has O(1) random access to any index — no pointer sweep is needed at all, just arithmetic on indices.

items.prompt_md: You're given two sorted arrays and need to merge them into one sorted array in O(n + m) time. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers, one per array, always advancing whichever points at the smaller current element
Fast & slow pointers
Hash set of visited nodes
Reverse pointers in place
items.answer: Two pointers, one per array, always advancing whichever points at the smaller current element
items.explanation_md: Trap: merging sorted sequences sounds like it could need a linked-list-specific trick, but two arrays with indices call for the array two-pointer merge walk, not fast/slow pointers or a dummy-head linked structure.

items.prompt_md: Given a singly linked list, you must return `True` if it reads the same forward and backward. You may use the middle-finding trick as a first step. Which approach fits for locating that middle?
options: Fast & slow pointers to find the middle, then reverse the second half for comparison
Copy nodes to an array first
Dummy head + merge
Hash set of visited nodes
items.answer: Fast & slow pointers to find the middle, then reverse the second half for comparison
items.explanation_md: Locating the midpoint without counting length is exactly the fast/slow pointer job — it's the natural first phase of the palindrome check before the second half gets reversed and compared.

items.prompt_md: You need to remove the nth node from the end of a singly linked list in one pass, without a prior pass to count the list's length. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers with a fixed n-node gap between them, both advancing until the lead pointer reaches the end
Reverse pointers in place
Dummy head alone, with no pointer gap
Copy nodes to an array first
items.answer: Two pointers with a fixed n-node gap between them, both advancing until the lead pointer reaches the end
items.explanation_md: Maintaining a constant n-node gap between two pointers is the same fast/slow-family idea used for k-from-the-end lookups — a single pass replaces the naive two-pass length-then-seek approach.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine, for a singly linked list that may or may not contain a cycle, the exact node where the cycle begins (not just whether one exists). Which approach fits?
options: Fast & slow pointers to find a meeting point inside the cycle, then reset one pointer to the head and advance both one step at a time until they meet again
Dummy head + merge
Reverse pointers in place
Copy nodes to an array first
items.answer: Fast & slow pointers to find a meeting point inside the cycle, then reset one pointer to the head and advance both one step at a time until they meet again
items.explanation_md: Finding the cycle's start extends the same two-speed sweep: after the pointers meet inside the cycle, the distance math works out so that walking one pointer from the head and the other from the meeting point, both at one step per move, converges exactly at the cycle's entry node.
